coredata.dc:title: Long-wavelength excitation of carbon dots with dual-organelle targeting capability for live-cell imaging via STED nanoscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hao
Guo, Jiaqing
Liu, Aikun
Shen, Xiaochun
Li, Jinlei
Weng, Xiaoyu
Liao, Changrui
He, Jun
Liu, Liwei
Wang, Yiping
Qu, Junle
Song, Jun
Yan, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Mitochondria and lysosomes play important roles in maintaining normal cellular metabolism; thus, visualizing their morphology, structure, and interactions in living cells at the nanometer scale is essential for further understanding their functions. However, this remains challenging because of the current limitations of fluorescent probes, including poor photobleaching resistance, low biocompatibility, and lack of multi-organelle targeting. Herein, we report the development of biocompatible nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CNDs) with long excitation wavelengths, stable fluorescence, and dual-organelle targeting capabilities. The CNDs enter cells predominantly via energy-dependent transport (including clathrin- and caveolae-mediated endocytosis) and free diffusion and subsequently target the interior mitochondria and lysosomes; furthermore, they enable the stimulated emission depletion imaging of these organelles in living cells with resolutions of up to 134 and 55 nm, respectively. This study provides practical guidance for designing dual-organelle targeting fluorescent probes for the dynamic super-resolution imaging of cellular systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-organelle targeting
Mitochondria
Lysosome
Super-resolution imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Examination of the CND cellular uptake pathways
Organelle targeting capability and photobleaching resistance of the CNDs
STED imaging of HeLa cells incubated with CNDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic showing the preparation process of the CNDs. (b) TEM image of the synthesized CNDs. (c) XPS full-scan profile of the CNDs. (d) C 1s and (e) N 1s XPS profiles of the CNDs. (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CNDs under different excitation wavelengths. (g) UV–vis absorption (violet line), excitation (red line, λex = 514 nm), and emission spectra (red dotted line, λem = 535 nm) of the CNDs in solution.

(a)–(c) Fluorescence stability of the synthesized CNDs with varying salt concentration (left), pH (center), and temperature (right). (d) Effect of different anions, cations and biologically relevant species (10 mM) on the CND fluorescence. (e) Viability of HeLa cells after exposure to different concentrations of CNDs for 24 h.

(a) CLSM images of living HeLa cells treated with the synthesized CNDs (15 μg/mL) for different incubation times. (b) CLSM images of HeLa cells before (control) and after treatment with various temperatures and pathway inhibitors. (c) Fluorescence intensities of the HeLa cells before (control) and after treatment with various temperatures and inhibitors. Scale bars = 20 μm.

(a) CLSM images of living HeLa cells treated with CNDs (excitation wavelength: 488 nm, emission spectrum: 500–550 nm) and LysoTracker Red (excitation wavelength: 644 nm, emission spectrum: 663–738 nm). (b) Corresponding fluorescence intensity profile analysis of the indicated area in Fig. 4a. (c) Fluorescence signal loss profile of the an CNDs d LysoTracker Red with laser irradiation time (laser power = 0.3 kW/cm2). (d) CLSM images of living HeLa cells treated with CNDs (excitation wavelength: 488 nm, emission spectrum: 500–550 nm) and MitoTracker Deep Red (excitation wavelength: 644 nm, emission spectrum: 663–738 nm). (e) Corresponding fluorescence intensity profile analysis of the indicated area in Fig. 4d. (f) Fluorescence signal loss profile of the CNDs and MitoTracker Deep Red with laser irradiation time.

(a) PL spectrum of the synthesized CNDs (λex = 513 nm). (b) Up-converted PL spectra of the CNDs after irradiation at wavelengths of 592, 660, and 775 nm. (c–f) Optical imaging of a live HeLa cell sample incubated with CNDs (scale bar = 10 μm). The cells were irradiated at wavelengths of 513, 592, 660, and 775 nm (left–right).

(a) STED nanoscopy images of a CND-labeled HeLa cell sample, captured at different scan times, upon combined exposure to excitation (λex = 513 nm, power = 1.0 μW) and depletion (λex = 660 nm, power = 39.6 mW) lasers over a period of 305 s (scale bar = 10 μm). (b) Fluorescence signal loss profile of the CND-labeled HeLa cells.

(a) CLSM images of living CND-labeled HeLa cells (scale bar = 20 μm). (b) Enlarged confocal image of area 1 (orange) in Fig. 7a (scale bar = 2 μm). (c) Normalized fluorescence intensity profile along the indicated line across the mitochondria in Fig. 7b. (d) STED image corresponding to the region shown in Fig. 7b (scale bar = 2 μm). (e) Normalized fluorescence intensity profile along the indicated line across the mitochondria in Fig. 7d. (f) Enlarged confocal image of area 2 (pink) in Fig. 7a (scale bar = 2 μm). (g) Normalized fluorescence intensity profile along the indicated line across the lysosome in Fig. 7(f). (h) STED image corresponding to the region shown in Fig. 7f (scale bar = 2 μm). (i) Normalized fluorescence intensity profile along the indicated line across the lysosome in Fig. 7h.
The synthetic route of dual-organelle targeting carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Long-wavelength excitation of carbon dots with dual-organelle targeting capability for live-cell imaging via STED nanoscopy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Radio Frequency Heterogeneous Integration(Shenzhen University), College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Radio Frequency Heterogeneous Integration(Shenzhen University)
College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering
Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Radio Frequency Heterogeneous Integration(Shenzhen University), College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mitochondria and lysosomes play important roles in maintaining normal cellular metabolism; thus, visualizing their morphology, structure, and interactions in living cells at the nanometer scale is essential for further understanding their functions. However, this remains challenging because of the current limitations of fluorescent probes, including poor photobleaching resistance, low biocompatibility, and lack of multi-organelle targeting. Herein, we report the development of biocompatible nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CNDs) with long excitation wavelengths, stable fluorescence, and dual-organelle targeting capabilities. The CNDs enter cells predominantly via energy-dependent transport (including clathrin- and caveolae-mediated endocytosis) and free diffusion and subsequently target the interior mitochondria and lysosomes; furthermore, they enable the stimulated emission depletion imaging of these organelles in living cells with resolutions of up to 134 and 55 nm, respectively. This study provides practical guidance for designing dual-organelle targeting fluorescent probes for the dynamic super-resolution imaging of cellular systems.

The nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CNDs) with Long-wavelength excitation for the visualization of lysosomes and mitochondria in the stimulated emission depletion imaging (STED) of live HeLa cells.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lysosomes and mitochondria are important organelles that maintain the normal physiological activities of cells; thus, their dysfunction can be associated with various diseases [1–5]. When maintaining intracellular homeostasis, lysosomes digest damaged organelles and recycle macromolecules and subsequently exhibit morphological changes [6,7]. The structure and morphology of mitochondria also change during cell metabolism into spherical, rod-like, or filamentous particles [8–10]. Furthermore, the interactions between mitochondria and lysosomes have been extensively explored, such as the fusion of the lysosomes with mitochondria and their contact [11–13]. Their contact promotes the transmission of materials and information. When their contacting function is disrupted, the occurrence of human-related diseases, such as Parkinson's and lysosomal storage–related diseases, is noted [14]. Therefore, their structural and morphological changes must be observed at the nanoscale for identifying their physiological function and the pathogenesis of the related diseases [8,15] [–] [18]. However, the optical diffraction limit of conventional microscopes limits the visual observation of nanoscale intracellular structures when using fluorescence imaging techniques.

In recent years, various super-resolution imaging techniques, including structured illumination microscopy, stochastic optical reconstruction microscopy (STORM), and stimulated emission depletion (STED), have enabled the observation of organelle structures and real-time dynamic processes with improved spatial resolution [19–22]. In particular, STED nanoscopy can perform fast, direct, and non-reconstructed imaging without any chemical modification of the sample [23–25]. For this application, STED probes should possess high photobleaching resistance, high depletion efficiency, and low saturation intensity [26–28]. Recently, a series of suitable probes has been designed and prepared for this purpose, including fluorescent proteins, organic fluorescent molecules, rare earth nanomaterials, perovskite nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots, and carbon dots [27,29–34]. However, these probes possess several shortcomings that must be addressed before their practical application. Semiconductor quantum dots are difficult to apply to live-cell imaging because of their strong cytotoxicity and inefficient organelle targeting. Meanwhile, p-conversion rare earth materials have long fluorescence lifetimes (up to the millisecond range) and limiting imaging speeds, which render the real-time observation of vital cellular activities difficult [35,36]. Furthermore, perovskite quantum dots cannot achieve specific marker imaging in vivo because they are easily destroyed in aqueous solutions. While organic fluorescence molecules do exhibit improved organelle targeting and membrane permeability, their low resistance to photobleaching limits their use in the in situ observation of cell dynamics [37,38]. Traditional carbon dots also have several disadvantages, such as short excitation wavelengths, low quantum yields (QY) in aqueous solutions, and limited single-organelle targeting [39].

For investigating lysosome–mitochondria interactions via STED, dual-organelle targeting probe is required to reduce the probe-incurred damage to the organelles during re-staining, and prevent phototoxicity to cells under multi-channel laser irradiation when observing the interaction between lysosomes and the mitochondria [40,41]. The probes must also possess long excitation wavelengths to reduce phototoxic damage during the initial excitation process [39,42,43]. Based on these criteria, carbon dots are the optimal candidate materials; this is because they possess abundant functional groups on their surfaces that may be exploited to modify their properties. In addition, heteroatom doping becomes one of the most commonly used methods to modify carbon dots to exhibit unique photoluminescence properties, low toxicity, and high-water solubility [44,45]. It is worth mentioning that the nitrogen-doping into carbon dots usually led to a red-shift of PL emission because of N– as an electron donor group [46,47]. And doped N can generate the defects sites, and improve the fluorescence properties of carbon dots resulting in increased fluorescence quantum yield [48,49]. More importantly, if carbon dots can double-target mitochondria and lysosomes, they should have the corresponding targeting functional groups. Studies have found that carbon dots containing contain carboxyl groups can target lysosomes through clathrin-mediated and caveolae-mediated endocytosis pathways [50]. Additionally, the rhodamine moiety is easily localized and aggregated in mitochondria [51]. So, the carbon dots containing rhodamine structure and carboxyl group can be prepared by using the tartaric acid and 3-aminophenol as raw materials (Scheme 1 ).

Based on the above the design strategy, biocompatible nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CNDs) with a dual-organelle targeting capability and long excitation wavelengths were successfully prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method using tartaric acid and 3-aminophenol as raw materials. After fabrication, the CNDs were tested to determine their fluorescence properties, stability, and cellular toxicity with HeLa cells. Subsequently, their potential cellular uptake pathways, applicability to the STED imaging of living HeLa cells, and organelle-resolving ability were studied.

The experimental materials were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Beijing Chemical Reagents. Before use, all solid samples and materials were dried under vacuum at 60 °C. Ultrapure water (18 MΩ/cm) was used for all experiments. PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) were also used for cell incubations and rinsing.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence spectrum (PL), UV–visible absorption spectrum (UV–Vis), Fluorescence life-time imaging microscopy (FLIM) image, Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) image and Stimulated emission depletion (STED) image of CNDs were carried out using FEI/Philips Tecnai G2F20 TWIN TEM, Bruker Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Hyperion), Thermo ESCALAB 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, Horiba Jobin Yvon luminescence spectrometer (Fluoro Max-4), GBC UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cintra 2020), FLIM image system (time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system with a Becker &Hickl SPC120 unit and Nikon Eclipse confocal microscope system), Nikon (A1R MP−), Carl Zeiss (LSM 800 with Airyscan) and Leica super-resolution microsystem (TCS SP8 STED 3X), respectively. The grain diameter distribution and Zeta potential of the N-CDs were collected with Zeta sizer Nano ZS, ZEN 3690, Malvern.

Tartaric acid (0.523 g, 3.48 mmol) and 3-diethylaminophenol (0.46 g, 2.78 mmol) were mixed in ultrapure water (30 mL) and placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was then heated to 180 °C for 24 h; once complete, the vessel was then cooled to 25 °C. The resulting red solution was filtered through an ultrafiltration membrane (0.22 μm) and a 500 Da dialysis membrane before use.

Several CND solutions (15 μg/mL) were prepared at different pH (2–10), temperatures (25–100 °C), ionic strengths (0.1–1.0 mM), and with different anion/cation combinations (salt solution concentration = 10 mM). After mixing, the CND solutions were allowed to stand for 10 min for stabilization, after which their fluorescence intensities were recorded.

The colorimetric 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) reduction method was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the prepared CNDs. HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and cultured in a standard medium at 37 °C for 24 h under a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Different concentrations of CNDs (10 μL) were then added to the cell-incubated wells, after which the plate was placed in a cell incubator at 35 °C for 24 h. Next, an acidified sodium dodecyl sulfate solution was used to lyse the HeLa cells. The absorbance of each well was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader. All measurements were performed in triplicate, and at least three independent experiments were performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of the CND cellular uptake pathways
ce:section.ce:para: Several inhibitors, including 2-deoxy-d-glucose (2-DG, ATP synthesis), chlorpromazine (CHP, clathrin-mediated endocytosis), genistein (GEN, caveolae-mediated endocytosis), methyl beta-cyclodextrin (MβCD, lipid raft-mediated endocytosis), and amiloride (AMI, macropinocytosis) were used to determine the CND internalization pathways. 30 mM 2-deoxy-d-glucose, 10 μM chlorpromazine, 200 μM genistein, 2 mM methyl beta-cyclodextrin, or 100 μM amiloride were added to the HeLa cells in DMEM and co-incubated in a cell incubator for 1 h at 37 °C. Next, the HeLa cells were washed three times with DMEM, and CND solutions (10 μL, 15 mg/mL) were subsequently co-incubated with the inhibitor-treated HeLa cells for 10 min at 25 °C. After washing three times with PBS, the HeLa cells were directly observed using via CLSM (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–550 nm).

For subcellular localization, commercial dyes (LysoTracker Red or MitoTracker Deep Red) were added to a 96-well plate incubated with HeLa cells as described above; this was then placed in a cell incubator at 35 °C for 30 min. The HeLa cell plate was then washed three times with PBS. Next, CND solutions (10 μL, 3 mg/mL) were added to the dye-treated HeLa cells, which were then incubated at 25 °C for 10 min. After washing three times with PBS, the HeLa cells were directly observed via CLSM (CNDs: λex/λem = 488/500–550 nm; LysoTracker Red: λex/λem = 644/663–738 nm; MitoTracker Deep Red: λex/λem = 644/663–738 nm).

For cell imaging, CND solutions (10 μL, 3 mg/mL) were co-incubated with HeLa cells in the cell culture medium (2 mL) for 10 min at 25 °C. The cells were then washed three times with PBS and measured via CLSM (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–550 nm) and STED (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–550 nm, depletion laser wavelength = 775 nm).

First, the CNDs were prepared by the hydrothermal reaction of tartaric acid and 3-diethylaminophenol (Fig. 1 a); here, the latter molecule acts as a source of nitrogen to dope the resulting carbon dots. As shown in the subsequent TEM images (Fig. 1b), CNDs synthesized in this fashion exhibit spherical morphologies and high dispersion. The size distribution of the CNDs (Fig. S1) indicates that the average nanodot size is approximately 4.9 nm.

To verify whether the nitrogen doping and forming rhodamine structure was successful, FTIR and XPS profiles were measured to study the functional group and elemental composition of the CND surfaces. As shown in Fig. S2, the FTIR spectrum of CNDs, the –OH stretching vibration at ∼3350 cm−1 is observed [52]. Then, the asymmetric and symmetric methylene (–CH2–) stretching vibrations was at 2970 and 2920 cm−1 absorbance peaks [53,54]. The two peaks at 1728 and 1404 cm−1 were attributed to the CO and C-O bonds of carboxyl groups [55]. The stretching vibration of C-N bonds were loaded at 1404 cm−1, respectively [56]. More importantly, the peaks at 1248 cm−1 of CNDs are attributed to the C–O–C stretching vibration of diphenyl ether groups in the rhodamine structure [57]. Moreover, to prove CNDs contained the rhodamine structure, the FTIR of Rhodamine B as control group was measured. As shown in Fig. S3, the characteristic peaks of Rhodamine B appeared in the FTIR spectra of as-prepared CNDs. So, the CNDs possess the rhodamine structure. To further determine the FTIR results, As shown in Fig. 1c, the XPS full-scan profile of the CNDs exhibits three peaks at 284, 400, and 530 eV, which can be attributed to C 1s (63.1%), N 1s (5.4%), and O 1s (31.5%), respectively. Narrow spectra of the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s regions were also measured (Fig. 1d and e and S4, respectively); here, the C 1s region exhibits three components corresponding to C-C/CC (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.4 eV), and CO (288.7 eV), while three nitrogen environments are observed in the N 1s region at 399.1 (Pyridine N), 400.4 (Pyrrolic N) and 401.3 eV (Graphitic N). Additionally, for the O 1s spectrum (Fig. S4), there were two peaks, with the one at 531.3 eV attributed to CO and the other located at 532.3 eV to the C–O/C-O-C bond. Based on the FTIR and XPS data, the CNDs not only possess many kinds of hydrophilic groups but also so have a rhodamine structure.

Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectroscopies were then used to study the optical properties of the CNDs. The fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 1f; here, the CNDs exhibit variable fluorescence depending on the excitation wavelength used. The optimum excitation and emission wavelengths are 513 and 535 nm, respectively (Fig. 1g), while the Stokes shift, fluorescence QY and fluorescence lifetime are 22 nm, 24.9% and 1.54 ns, respectively (Figs. S5–S6). Three absorption peaks at 274, 386, and 514 nm can also be observed in the CND UV–vis absorption spectrum; these can attributed to the π-π*, n-π*, and CN absorptions of pyridinic and pyrrolic N, respectively [58], thus corroborating the N 1s XPS results. The zeta potential of the CNDs was found to be +6.00 mV, indicating that the CND surfaces are positively charged (Fig. S7).

To further examine whether the CNDs could be used as fluorescence probes in live-cell imaging, their fluorescence stability and cytotoxicity were investigated. Fig. 2 a–c shows the variation of fluorescence intensity of the CNDs with ionic strength, pH, and temperature. As the pH and ionic strength are varied, the intensity does not change significantly, indicating that the CNDs are resistant to these environmental factors. A gradual decrease in intensity is seen as the temperature is increased from 20 to 90 °C; however, the change is relatively minor, amounting to an intensity reduction of under 11% over the studied range. Furthermore, changing the solvated anions and cations has little effect on the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2d). Moreover, Cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH) and reactive oxygen species (ROS) as biological thiols widely exist in cell. So, their effect on the fluorescence property of CNDs is also studied (Fig. 2d). These results show that the CNDs possess stable fluorescence and thus may be utilized in a variety of biological environments. The cytotoxicity of the CNDs to living HeLa cells was also examined as a function of the CND concentration. After lysing, the HeLa cell viability upon CND exposure remains high with increasing concentration (Fig. 2e). Furthermore, the highest concentration studied (400 μg/mL) exhibits an approximately 86% viability, indicating that high CND concentrations can be used with only minor cytotoxicity to the HeLa cells. These results therefore suggest that the CNDs possess considerable biocompatibility and are therefore suitable fluorescent probes for live-cell imaging.

The optimum incubation time and cellular uptake pathways of the CNDs were investigated by studying how the nanodots were incorporated into the HeLa cells. Fig. 3 a shows CLSM images of living HeLa cells incubated with the CNDs for different durations (0.5, 1, 2, and 10 min). Here, the HeLa cells exhibit maximum fluorescence after 10 min of CND exposure at 25 °C, indicating that this is the optimum incubation time. Next, to identify the primary uptake pathways for the CNDs, the uptake rate was studied as a function of temperature and the functional pathway inhibitor used; here, the inhibitors 2-DG, CHP, GEN, MβCD, and AMI were selected [58]. The uptake rates were determined based on the increase in the CND fluorescence intensity with time. The CLSM results are shown in Fig. 3b and c. Fluorescence signals can be detected in the living HeLa cells at 4 °C, which suggests that CNDs can enter the cells via free diffusion. Moreover, the intensity increases at 37 °C, indicating that the rate of CND absorption is related to the energy in the cells. When the HeLa cells are exposed to the 2-DG, CHP, GEN, MβCD, and AMI inhibitors, the uptake rate decreases by 21%, 32%, 19%, 10%, and 1%, respectively. Consequently, the results indicate that the CNDs enter cells predominantly via energy-dependent transport (clathrin- and caveolae-mediated endocytosis) and free diffusion mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organelle targeting capability and photobleaching resistance of the CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the organelle targeting capability and fluorescence stability of the CNDs, two types of commercial dyes (LysoTracker Red and MitoTracker Deep Red) were used for colocalized cell imaging. As shown in Fig. 4 a and d, the green fluorescent signal of the CNDs overlaps considerably with the signals of both the commercial probes. This can also be seen when comparing selected fluorescence intensity profiles of the CNDs to those of the probes (Fig. 4b and e) in the linear region of interest; as these probes attach specifically to lysosomes or mitochondria, this result verifies the dual-organelle targeting capability of the synthesized CNDs. Because mitochondria generally possess negative transmembrane potentials between 180 and 200 mV, the positively charged CNDs containing the rhodamine structure can be absorbed into the mitochondria through electrostatic attraction [59]. Moreover, the surfaces of the CNDs contain amino and carboxyl zwitterionic groups, which can target lysosomes via clathrin- and caveolae-mediated endocytosis [60]. As shown in Fig. 4c and f, after constant irradiation with a high intensity laser (laser power = 0.3 kW/cm2) for 600 s, the fluorescence intensities of both LysoTracker Red and Mito-TrackerDeep Red decrease by 59 and 60%, respectively. However, the CNDs retain approximately 82% of their initial intensity after irradiation, thus indicating their excellent photostability. The synthesized CNDs can therefore enable high-fidelity stable imaging when targeting lysosomes and mitochondria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: STED imaging of HeLa cells incubated with CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we investigated whether the synthesized CNDs could be applied to super-resolution imaging using a commercial STED system. To obtain high quality super-resolved images, the optimal depletion beam must be determined. For the LEICA SP8 STED system used in this study, 592, 660, and 775 nm beams may be utilized (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b shows the fluorescence spectra of the CNDs obtained under different depletion beam excitations. The CNDs emit the weakest spontaneous fluorescence under excitation with the 660 nm laser, thus indicating that this was the optimal depletion beam for the subsequent STED imaging. To verify this, the CND-treated live HeLa cell samples were imaged when irradiated by the excitation or depletion lasers (Fig. 5c–f). Here, large fluorescence signals are detected after irradiation at 592 or 775 nm, while no signal is observed when the 660 nm depletion beam is used. Because spontaneous fluorescence signals hinder the realization of the depletion ratio and reduces the STED resolution, the 660 nm laser was therefore selected as the depletion beam in the subsequent experiments.

Once the optimal depletion beam was selected, the CNDs were further examined for their suitability as fluorescent probes for STED imaging. To this end, an anti-photobleaching experiment was conducted by irradiating samples of CND-treated HeLa cells with the combined STED excitation (λex = 513 nm, power = 1.0 μW) and depletion (power = 39.6 mW) lasers for 305 s (Fig. 6 a and b). During the exposure period, the fluorescence intensity of the resulting STED image is reduced by 16%, indicating the excellent stability of the CNDs when applied to STED imaging; therefore, it was determined that the as-prepared CNDs were suitable for application in live-cell imaging.

Based on their excellent photostability and dual-organelle targeting specificity, the CNDs were examined as probes for direct STED imaging of lysosomes and mitochondria (Fig. 7 ). Fig. 7a shows the CND-labeled living HeLa cells when observed using CLSM; clearly, the CNDs are absorbed and concentrated in the lysosomes and mitochondria, but the fluorescence images are unclear owing to the optical diffraction limit (lateral resolution >200 nm). As shown in Fig. 7b and f (corresponding to regions 1 and 2 in Fig. 7a), detailed information on the organelles cannot be obtained in confocal mode. By comparison, the STED images display the mitochondria and lysosomes as discontinuous spots, thus exhibiting a higher imaging resolution (Fig. 7d and h). The full width at half maximum of the mitochondria fluorescence signal in the STED image is 135 nm, a 1.7-fold enhancement over that of confocal imaging (Fig. 7c and e). A similar improvement is also observed for the STED imaging of the lysosomes, which exhibits an imaging resolution of 55 nm (Fig. 7g and i). These results therefore indicate that the fabricated CNDs are highly suitable as probes for STED imaging of lysosomes and mitochondria.

In summary, CNDs with long excitation wavelengths were designed for the simultaneous live-cell imaging of lysosomes and mitochondria. First, the CNDs were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal method, and subsequent characterization showed that the carbon dots were successfully N-doped and possessed suitable water solubility. The CNDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility and optical stability over long irradiation times, thus reducing potential damage to cells during imaging. The CNDs as fluorescent probes were also shown to discretely label mitochondria and lysosomes via the clathrin- and caveolae-mediated endocytic pathways and could freely diffuse into living cells. Furthermore, the experimental data indicated that the synthesized CNDs were suitable as probes for STED imaging, where they produced high-fidelity images of lysosomes and mitochondria with improved resolutions of up to 55 and 135 nm, respectively. These CNDs therefore possess considerable potential for practical application in the nanometer-scale visualization of lysosomes and mitochondria in living cells. This research also provides guidance for future design strategies for specific dual-organelle targeting probes for live-cell imaging; furthermore, these findings demonstrate that CNDs can serve as efficient alternatives to commercial STED imaging probes.

Hao Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jiaqing Guo: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Aikun Liu: Validation. Xiaochun Shen: Validation. Jinlei Li: Validation. Xiaoyu Weng: Formal analysis. Changrui Liao: Formal analysis. Jun He: Formal analysis. Liwei Liu: Investigation. Yiping Wang: Methodology, Resources. Junle Qu: Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Funding acquisition. Jun Song: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Wei Yan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled, “Long-wavelength excitation of carbon dots with dual-organelle targeting capability for live-cell imaging via STED nanoscopy”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFF0502900), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62175161/61835009/62127819/62071306/61975127), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M702240), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022A1515011954/2023A1515011499/2023A1515011114), the Key Project of Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2021ZDZX2013), the Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (JCYJ20210324095810028/JCYJ20210324095613036/JCYJ20220531102807017) and Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Photonics and Biophotonics (ZDSYS20210623092006020). We also thank the Photonics Research Center of Shenzhen University and Lin (Ph. D) for technical assistance with the Leica SP8 confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111383.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Autophagy fights disease through cellular self-digestion
